15. Random Walks

15.1. [REVIEW] Markov Processes

We opened by introducing Scotland yard as a Markov game and gambler’s ruin as a Markov process.

Definition 8. A Markov process is a memoryless discrete stochastic process , where for all . Let denote the transitions at time .

Then we can represent with a graph . Some further notes:

  • is partioned into SCCs called communicating classes, which are either recurrent or transient.

  • A stationary state is a row vector satisfying where is the transition matrix, defined such that .

    • Warning: to ascribe additional meaning to a stationary state, you require extra properties like ergodicity. Simply interpret it mathematically.
    • In any finite Markov chain, probability leaks out of transient communicating classes so that stationary states assign them zero probability.
      Sketch. Suppose you start in a transient class with nonzero probability. Then the probability you are in in later must be smaller, since you have a nonzero probability of leaving and zero probability of returning. Hence you cannot have started in a stationary state.

Definition 9 (Periodicity).

  • The period of a vertex in a directed graph is the gcd of the lengths of all directed cycles in its communicating class in the graph.
  • A directed graph is said to be periodic if some communicating class is periodic.

Remark. For an undirected graph, the periodicity can be described by bipartiteness, since the existence of an odd cycle implies aperiodicity.

Theorem 9 (Fundamental Theorem of Markov Chains). LEt be a time-homogenous Markov process, where for all and be its Markov chain. If is strongly connected and aperiodic, then every random walk on converges to a unique stationary distribution.

We build up the proof by splitting it up into the following three facts:

  1. Proposition 6. All finite Markov chains have a stationary distribution.

    Sketch. Write out the dual and show that it is feasible. Check GPT: https://chatgpt.com/c/692caae2-1cb8-8333-a8e9-72267921dc68

  2. Theorem 10 (Unique stationary distribution). For a time-homogenous Markov process, if is strongly connected then it has a unique stationary distribution.

    Example 6. Connected undirected graphs are strongly connected and hence have a unique stationary distribution. It turns out it’s the uniform distribution normalized by its degree sequence.

    Sketch. Apply uniform balance, with .

  3. Theorem 11 (Convergence of random walks). If is aperiodic, then every random walk on converges (to a stationary distribution).

    More powerfully, this holds if the recurrent communicating classes (no outgoing edges) are aperiodic.

Example 7. Lazy random walks are random walks in indirected graphs where we have nonzero probability to stay at the current vertex. This maeks the graph non-bipartite and aperiodic, satisfying FTOMC.
Example 8 (Gambler's Ruin). Gambling until you go bankrupt or reach a payout is a random walk with three communicating classes: the two boundaries plus the middle.

Sketch. All stationary distributions are in the form and . Starting at in the middle, the converged distribution is . Note that this doesn’t mean the balance spends half the time broke or rich. We need a notion of ergodicity for long-run average.

15.2. Classification of Markov Chains

We consider the various pairs of possibilities regarding the structure and behavior of a Markov chain:

  1. strongly connected (A) or not strongly connected (B)
  2. aperiodic (A) or periodic (B)
  3. every random walk converges (A) or some but not all random walks converge (B)
  4. unique stationary distribution (A) or non-unique stationary distribution (B)
The FTOMC prohibits 7 types in the form A__B and _AB_. It is easy to come up with examples for the remaining 9 except ABAA. This last case ABAA is impossible:
Proposition 7. There is no finite Markov chain that is strongly connected, is periodic, and every random walk converges to a unique stationary distribution (ABAA).

Sketch. Pick a node . By periodicity, all return paths must have lengths . This fixes the path lengths modulo to intermediate nodes. That is, suppose there is a state reachable from in two different number of steps modulo . Then we can construct two different return lengths, and . Their difference is which is not . This yields a contradiction. Thus we can partition the graph into classes of the same distance to , such that transitions are in the form

(36)

Then, the state does not converge.

15.3. Example: MCMC

Often we encounter probability distributions that are computationally intractable to sample from. One approach is to design a Markov chain such that its unique stationary distribution is the desired distribution. This is a family of algorithms known as Monte Carlo Markov Chain (MCMC).

Then we can sample using random walks (e.g. Metropolis-Hastings) in which we hope that all random walks converge to that distribution fast. Other MCMC techniques such as HMC, Gibbs, and NUTS do other stuff.

15.4. Prelude to Metropolis-Hastings

Metropolis-Hastings (MH) is one such algorithm that samples from an arbitrary probability distribution up to normalization. To setup the problem, let be a random variable whose distribution is supported on some set . Suppose we have access to a function outputs unnormalized probability densities so that we want to divide by . The difficulty is that may be too large making it computationally intractable to sum all the terms necessary to compute . MH allows us to sample from without solving for .

For example, in Bayesian statistics the posterior is known to normalization, and we can sample it via random walks to estimate things like the mean and variance of a parameter.

Before describing the algorithm, we define the notion of reversibility and go through some examples.

15.5. Reversibility

Definition 10. A stochastic process is said to be reversible if and only if the distribution of the random variables is identical to the distribution of for all .

Let’s apply it to Markov chains.

Proposition 8 (Detailed Balance Conditions). A time-homogenous Markov process described by a transition matrix is said to be reversible if and only if it possesses a stationary distribution such that satisfied detailed balance conditions:

(37)

In particular, if then the resulting process is reversible.

Sketch. Reversibility with and arbitrary implies stationarity. Reversibility with and implies detailed balance.

To show that stationarity plus detailed balance imply reversibility, write the likelihood of and and apply conditional probability with detailed balance.

Proposition 9. If satisfies detailed balance wrt to , then is a stationary distribution.
Proof

Suppose . Want to show . I.e.

□

Facts:

  • Detailed balance conditions are strictly stronger than stationarity. For example, consider a Markov chain that is a loop around a polygon, which doesn’t satisfy DB.

  • The following two facts:

    1. Cyclic property with transition probabilities.
    2. Symmetry transition matrix is reversible and admits uniform stationary distribution. More generally a doubly stochastic matrix has a uniform stationary distribution.
Example 9 (Bernoulli Arrivals and Departures). [TODO] Nontrivial that this is reversible. The stationary distribution is geometric.

15.6. Example: Independent Set

An independent set is a set of vertices that don’t share an edge. The complement of an independent set is a vertex cover and vice versa. Hence, deceiding whether there exists an independent set of size is NP complete.

As a motivating example for MCMC, consider the probleem of uniformly sampling independent sets of an unweighted undirected graph. The space of all sets can be exponential in and counting them is NP-hard, so how do we do this efficiently?

Randomized Algorithm

  • Let

  • Repeat for steps:

    • Sample, uniformly at random, a vertex .
    • If , set with probability .
    • If and shares no edge with vertices in , set with probability .
  • Output .

This is clearly a Markov process and can be represented by a random walk of length over a Markov chain. It is strongly connected (go from to empty to ) and aperiodic as there are self-loops. Hence by the FTOMC, all random walks converge to its unique stationary distribution. It suffices to show this is the uniform distribution on the space of all independent sets of . We show it satisfies the detailed balance condition that for any two indepent sets and . This is trivial, as both terms equal .

Yay! We didn’t need to know the exact huge chain, but rather perform a random walk on it.

15.7. Example: Sampling Graph Colorings

Now let’s sample a valid -coloring of uniformly at random. Such space can be as large as .

Randomized algorithm for when is large enough
Assume that where is the highest degree of any vertex in .

  • Let , an arbitray -coloring of .

  • Repeat for steps:

    • Uniformly sample a vertex and a color .
    • If the new coloring is valid, replace it. Otherwise leave as is.

The elments of the Markov chain are the -colorings. It’s clearly aperiodic. Our assumption also implies strong connectedness, i.e. we can color to one vertex at a time: [REVIEW] to make match , color that vertex to some random valid one, color the neighbors, and then color it.

Suppose is a distribution function and we know .

  1. Define a proposal distribution for transition densities. Interpret as .

  2. Define the Markov process described by the transition matrix where

    (38)

    for and

If and are both strictly positive, then the Markov chain is strongly connected and aperiodic. Hence it sufices to show that has as a stationary distribution. This follows from the observation that if and are both not equal to zero, then at least one of and must equal one. Apply casework and show that it satisfies detailed balance: .

In pratice, we allow a burn in phase , and take as correlated samples of . The correlation is okay, since by ergodicity the sample estimate for approaches the expected value. Let be an indicator function for a small slice of the distribution to argue that the sampled distribution converges.

Remark. Recall rejection and importance sampling:

MH can be viewed as a form a local rejection sampling. Don’t think to much about trying to make this analogy tight.

15.9. [REVIEW] Mixing Time via Coupling

Mixing time quantifies how long it a random walk takes to converge to a staionary distribution. We will not go over formalism.

Let and be two time-homogenous Markov processes. A coupling between and is a way to perform correlated random walks on and . Consider the case where and but the random walks have different initial distributions. One begins and stays at the stationary distribution. The other begins at the distribution whose mixing time we are trying to analyze. When the random walks collide and they agree onwards because they are correlated. We use this yield a constructive upper bound to mixing time.

L21 has such example on a graph of size 4. We can model both walks by sampling 2 bits. To make them correlated, we use the same choices of bits.

To elaborate, we want to compare two probability distributions where is the total variation distance, largest absolute difference between probabilities. The coupling inequality implies . Henc we take the min over all couplings, in which

(39)

Hence so we can argue for large enough the distributions are the same.
